segments.text: Say yes first and learn later. Then I
had just moved to [music] New York to
start my degree. As any other student, I
was supposed to take up a [music]
part-time job as a student admissions
guy. My senior coworker comes up to me
and [music] says, "If there's this
campus tour that's going to happen today
and we don't [music] have anybody to do
it. Can you be the substitute?" And my
first reaction was of course absolutely
not. But then I stopped myself in my
steps [music] and I thought, you know
what? Let me say yes first and I'll
learn how to do it later. I'll figure it
out. That tour went phenomenally well
[music] to my own surprise. I acquired
three skills, three critical [music]
skills. The first was the skill of small
talk. How to make small talk with
strangers. The second skill was how to
present any information in an attractive
way [music] in a way that is attractive
to other people that can actually
influence their [music] decisions. And
the third and the most important one was
how to keep a crowd together. [music]
Being that if you have the choice to
seek discomfort, to go after [music]
discomfort, and there's learning
promised at the other end of it, that's
where you go.
